worth_it_explanation: The conversion window is one setting per conversion action, and it decides how many conversions LinkedIn reports and how much credit the platform claims. The checks are quick to run from the conversion's settings in Campaign Manager, and they need no tag changes. Reading the click and view window lengths against how a real buyer behaves tells you whether the conversion count reflects the campaign's actual influence or a window that is either too generous or too tight. The fix is a setting change, and the count it produces is more honest from the next reporting period forward.
intro: LinkedIn attributes a conversion to an ad within a conversion window, the lookback period that runs from a click or an impression to the conversion. There are two windows, a click-through window for members who clicked the ad and a view-through window for members who only saw it, and each can be set to 1, 7, 30, or 90 days. These checks confirm that the view-through window is not crediting impressions that other channels earned and that the click-through window is long enough to capture conversions the click genuinely influenced.

audit_checks.title: A long view-through window inflates the conversion count
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A view-through conversion credits an impression the member never clicked, so a long view-through window assigns LinkedIn credit for conversions that other channels may have driven. The reported conversion volume reads higher than the campaign's real influence, and decisions about budget and bidding lean on a number that overstates LinkedIn's contribution.

educate.base:
A view-through conversion records when a member saw a LinkedIn ad, did not click it, and later converted within the view-through window. LinkedIn counts that conversion against the ad even though the member's path to converting may have run through search, email, a direct visit, or another channel entirely. The longer the view-through window, the more of these unclicked impressions stay eligible for credit.

LinkedIn lets you set the view-through window to 1, 7, 30, or 90 days, separately from the click-through window. A 90-day view-through window keeps an impression eligible for credit for three months, so a member who saw one ad in March and converted in May still adds to LinkedIn's reported count. When the goal of the audit is to understand what LinkedIn actually drove, a window that wide can claim conversions the platform only brushed against.

The choice is a judgment about how much credit an unclicked impression deserves. A few questions help frame it:
  • How likely is it that a single impression, with no click, moved the member toward converting
  • Does the reported view-through volume look proportionate to the click-through volume, or does it dominate it
  • Are the same conversions being credited to other channels in their own reporting
You may want to confirm the view-through window length against how the rest of the measurement stack attributes the same conversions. A view-through window that is shorter than the click-through window, or view-through tracking turned off for conversions where an impression alone is weak evidence, can bring the reported count closer to LinkedIn's real influence.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a considered or business-to-business store, the purchase cycle is long enough that a 90-day view-through window can keep crediting old impressions against late purchases. You may want to confirm the view-through window matches how long an unclicked impression could plausibly influence a buyer, rather than the longest available setting.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-capture site, where the conversion is a form submission rather than a sale, a long view-through window often inflates lead counts with members who saw an ad once and filled the form for an unrelated reason. You may want to confirm the view-through window reflects how much an impression alone moves a lead, and consider a shorter window or view-through off for low-intent conversions.

investigate.intro: Confirm the view-through window length set on each conversion in Campaign Manager and compare the view-through conversion volume against the click-through volume.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: In Campaign Manager, open the account, then select 'Analyze' and 'Conversion tracking' to list the account's conversions.
Select a conversion and open its settings. Note the click-through window and the view-through window, each set to 1, 7, 30, or 90 days.

Return to the 'Conversions & leads' columns in the campaign reporting and read the view-through conversions against the click-through conversions for the same date range. Note any conversion where the view-through volume dominates the click-through volume.

ga4.label: Google Analytics 4
steps: Open a conversion report and filter to the same conversions LinkedIn is tracking, segmented by the channel or source that GA4 credits.

Compare the conversions GA4 attributes to LinkedIn against the volume LinkedIn reports for the same period. A large gap, where LinkedIn claims many more conversions, points to view-through credit that other channels are also counting.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per conversion, the view-through window length, the click-through window length, and the ratio of view-through to click-through conversions over a representative date range. A conversion whose view-through window is set to a length proportionate to the conversion's intent, with view-through volume that does not overwhelm click-through volume, passes. A conversion with a 90-day or otherwise long view-through window paired with view-through volume that dominates the click-through count is a fail recorded with the conversion name and the two window lengths.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the view-through and click-through window lengths and the view-through-to-click ratio for each conversion above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s LinkedIn conversions use a long view-through window, which credits impressions members saw but never clicked. A view-through conversion assigns LinkedIn credit for a conversion that other channels may have driven, so the reported volume reads higher than the campaign's real influence and budget decisions lean on an overstated count. {{voice_recommend}} setting the view-through window to a length that matches how much an unclicked impression could plausibly move the conversion, keeping it shorter than the click-through window, and turning view-through tracking off for conversions where an impression alone is weak evidence. After the change, compare the view-through volume against the click-through volume and against how the rest of the measurement stack attributes the same conversions.

audit_checks.title: The click-through window is too short for a long consideration cycle
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A click-through window that closes before a typical decision completes drops conversions the click genuinely influenced. The reported return runs lower than the campaign earned, and a campaign that is working can look underperforming because its conversions land after the window has closed.

educate.base:
A click-through conversion records when a member clicked a LinkedIn ad and then converted within the click-through window. LinkedIn lets you set that window to 1, 7, 30, or 90 days. If the window closes before the member finishes deciding, the conversion happens but LinkedIn no longer attributes it to the click, so the campaign that earned the click never sees the result in its reporting.

Business-to-business purchases on LinkedIn often run a long consideration cycle, where weeks pass between the first click and a signed contract or a completed sign-up. A 7-day click-through window on a conversion that typically takes a month to complete drops the conversions that land in weeks two through four. The campaign looks weaker than it is, and that distorted return can pull budget away from a campaign that is actually driving demand.

The right window length follows the real time from click to conversion, not a default. A few inputs help you judge it:
  • The typical lag between a member's first ad click and the conversion, drawn from the analytics platform or the customer relationship management (CRM) system
  • Whether the conversion is an early signal, such as a content download, or a late one, such as a demo request or a purchase
  • Whether the reported conversions cluster near the start of the window or trail off well before it closes
You may want to confirm the click-through window against the measured time-to-conversion before deciding it is too short. A window that matches the consideration cycle captures the conversions the click influenced; one set well below it leaves earned conversions uncounted.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a considered or business-to-business store, the time from an ad click to a purchase can run weeks, so a short click-through window drops sales the click started. You may want to confirm the window length against the measured time from first click to purchase rather than a low default.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-capture site, the dominant case on LinkedIn, the conversion is a form submission that can follow the click after a stretch of research. You may want to confirm the click-through window covers the typical lag from click to form submission, lengthening it where members tend to convert well after the first click.

investigate.intro: Confirm the click-through window length on each conversion in Campaign Manager and compare it against the measured time from click to conversion.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: In Campaign Manager, open the account, then select 'Analyze' and 'Conversion tracking' to list the account's conversions.
Select a conversion and open its settings. Note the click-through window, set to 1, 7, 30, or 90 days, and which step in the buying process the conversion represents.
For each conversion, note whether the window length looks proportionate to a typical decision cycle for that step, flagging short windows on late-stage conversions such as demo requests or purchases.

ga4.label: Google Analytics 4
steps: Open a path or conversion report and measure the typical lag between a LinkedIn ad click and the conversion for the same conversions LinkedIn tracks.

Compare that measured lag against each conversion's click-through window in Campaign Manager. A window shorter than the typical lag points to conversions LinkedIn is dropping because they land after the window closes.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per conversion, the click-through window length, the conversion's stage in the buying process, and the measured time from click to conversion where the analytics platform or CRM can supply it. A conversion whose click-through window covers the typical time-to-conversion passes. A conversion with a click-through window shorter than the measured lag, especially on a late-stage conversion, is a fail recorded with the conversion name, the window length, and the measured lag.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the click-through window length and the measured time from click to conversion for each conversion above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs a click-through window shorter than its consideration cycle on one or more LinkedIn conversions. When the window closes before a typical decision completes, conversions the click genuinely influenced land outside the window and go unattributed, so the reported return runs lower than the campaign earned and a working campaign can look underperforming. {{voice_recommend}} lengthening the click-through window to match the measured time from first click to conversion, drawn from the analytics platform or the CRM, and giving late-stage conversions such as demo requests or purchases a longer window than early signals. After the change, confirm the reported conversions begin to capture the ones that previously landed past the window.
